丁.origin: 丁 looks like a small nail with a flat top; it also means a grown man.

丈.origin: 丈 means an adult man or husband, and is also an old unit of length.

丑.origin: 丑 means ugly; remember the opposite of pretty.

世.origin: 世 once pictured leaves on a branch and now means generation or the world.

丘.origin: 丘 shows the shape of two small hills side by side.

丙.origin: 丙 is the third of the ten heavenly stems, used for counting like A, B, C.

sentence.en: He got a grade of C.

丛.origin: 丛 shows many plants 从 growing close together as a bush or cluster.

sentence.en: There are bees in the flowers.

丝.origin: 丝 is the simple form of 絲, showing fine threads of silk.

sentence.en: This silk scarf is very soft.

丸.origin: 丸 pictures a hand holding a little round pellet or ball.

丹.origin: 丹 shows a red pellet from a mine and means a red color.

为.origin: 为 means for or because of, telling why we do something.

主.origin: 主 pictures a lamp with a bright flame; it means owner or master.

乃.origin: 乃 is an old word meaning then or namely.

sentence.en: Failure is the mother of success.

乍.origin: 乍 means suddenly or at first, as in something happening all at once.

sentence.en: The weather is suddenly cold, suddenly hot.

乏.origin: 乏 means lacking or tired, as if running out of energy.

乐.origin: 乐 is the simple form of 樂 and means happy or music.

乒.origin: 乒 pictures a ball flying one way; with 乓 it makes ping-pong.

乓.origin: 乓 pictures a ball flying the other way; with 乒 it makes ping-pong.

乔.origin: 乔 means tall and lofty, like a person standing on stilts.

sentence.en: There are many tall trees in the park.

乖.origin: 乖 means well-behaved and obedient, often said to good children.

乘.origin: 乘 pictures feet climbing up; it means to ride or to multiply.

sentence.en: We take a bus to school.

乙.origin: 乙 looks like a curving swallow and is the second heavenly stem.

乱.origin: 乱 means messy or chaotic, like a tangled mess.

乳.origin: 乳 shows a mother caring for a baby; it means milk or breast.

了.origin: 了 looks like a swaddled baby; it marks that something is finished.

予.origin: 予 means to give, like handing something to another person.

亏.origin: 亏 means to lose or to fall short of what is owed.

sentence.en: In business you sometimes lose money.

互.origin: 互 pictures two hooks linking together; it means mutually.

井.origin: 井 pictures the wooden frame around a water well.

sentence.en: There is a well in the village.

亚.origin: 亚 means second or sub, and is short for Asia (亚洲).

亟.origin: 亟 means urgently, when something must be done right away.

亢.origin: 亢 means high or proud, standing up tall.

sentence.en: His singing is loud and high.

亥.origin: 亥 is the last of the twelve earthly branches, linked to the pig.

sentence.en: The hour of hai is at night.

亦.origin: 亦 means also or too, adding one more thing.

sentence.en: He is a student, and so am I.

亩.origin: 亩 is a Chinese unit for measuring fields of land.

sentence.en: This plot of land is three mu.

享.origin: 享 means to enjoy or to share something good.

京.origin: 京 pictures a tall building on a hill; it means capital city.

亭.origin: 亭 pictures a little open pavilion where people rest.

sentence.en: There is a pavilion in the park.

亮.origin: 亮 means bright, like a lit lamp shining.

亿.origin: 亿 means one hundred million, a very large number.

sentence.en: China has many hundreds of millions of people.

仁.origin: 仁 pairs a person 亻 with two 二, meaning kindness between people.

仅.origin: 仅 means only or merely, just this much and no more.

仆.origin: 仆 means a servant, a person who serves others.

sentence.en: In old times rich people had servants.

仇.origin: 仇 means enemy or hatred between people.

介.origin: 介 pictures a person between two lines; it means to be in between.

仓.origin: 仓 is the simple form of 倉, a place to store grain.

sentence.en: The grain is kept in the warehouse.

仗.origin: 仗 means to rely on, or a battle one fights.

sentence.en: They do not want to go to war.

仙.origin: 仙 pairs a person 亻 with a mountain 山, meaning an immortal who lives in the hills.

sentence.en: There are immortals in the story.

仞.origin: 仞 is an old unit of length, about the height of a person.

仟.origin: 仟 is the formal way to write one thousand (千).

sentence.en: The receipt says five thousand yuan.
